title: Forming a sheet from nettle fibre
subtitle: Preparing and using common stinging nettle as a paper pulp
excerpt: Stinging nettle (Urtica dioica) has been used as a textile and paper fibre for centuries. Retted and boiled, the stem fibres yield a long, strong pulp that forms sheets with a slightly greenish-yellow tint and a natural texture different from cotton linter or recycled paper pulp. The nettles can be harvested from any garden; the retting and beating are done at home.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943), Hunter discusses plant fibre preparation including bast fibres such as nettle and flax. The general process of retting, cooking, and beating plant fibres for papermaking is documented in multiple pre-1928 craft and technical publications.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of soaking plant fibres in water to soften and begin to separate the cellulose fibre bundles from the surrounding plant tissue. Water-retting takes 1 to 2 weeks; dew-retting (leaving stems on the ground outside) takes 3 to 4 weeks.

Cellulose fibre found in the stem of certain plants, nettle, flax, hemp, and ramie are all bast fibres. The fibre runs lengthways along the stem and must be separated from the woody core by retting and beating before it can be used for paper or textiles.

The mechanical treatment of soaked pulp to break down the fibre bundles into individual fibres. In home papermaking, a kitchen blender substitutes for the traditional Hollander beater. More beating produces a smoother, stronger, less textured sheet.

attrs.title: Handling nettles
attrs.body: Harvest with gloves and long sleeves. The sting goes after cooking, but the fresh plant stings. Use a separate pot; the pigments can stain.

content.text: , the usable cellulose runs in the stem, not the leaf. The leaves add a greenish tint to the pulp but contribute little fibre. Cut the stems in late summer when they are at their most fibrous and dry them in a bundle before

items.name: Dried stinging nettle stems, any quantity, 100 g dry weight makes approximately 20 A5 sheets
items.substitutions: Flax or hemp stems; dried iris leaves; recycled tissue paper for a quick practice pulp

items.name: Soda ash (sodium carbonate) or wood ash lye, for cooking
items.substitutions: Washing soda (the same chemical); baking soda produces a slightly weaker cook

content.text: Ret the dried stems: soak them in a bucket of water for 1 to 2 weeks until the outer plant tissue begins to soften and the fibre bundles separate slightly. Change the water every few days.

Drain and transfer the retted stems to a pot. Cover with fresh water and add soda ash. Simmer for 2 to 3 hours until the stems are soft and the outer tissue has loosened from the fibre. Drain and rinse thoroughly in clean water.
the cooked fibre in small batches in the papermaking blender with plenty of water until the fibre is fully broken down. The pulp should look like thin porridge with no visible fibre bundles.

Dilute the beaten nettle pulp in the vat at approximately 1 part pulp to 20 parts water. Agitate and pull a sheet in the same way as cotton linter or recycled paper. The nettle fibres are longer than cotton linter fibres and may produce a slightly uneven first sheet; agitate the vat between each pull.

items.symptom: The sheet has a dark green or brown tint
items.cause: Leaf material in the pulp; insufficient washing after cooking.
items.fix: Wash the cooked fibre thoroughly in multiple changes of water before beating. Sort out as much leaf material as possible before retting.

items.symptom: The sheet tears easily when wet
items.cause: The fibre was not beaten long enough.
items.fix: Beat in shorter batches for longer. Nettle fibre needs more beating than cotton linter to achieve the bonding needed for a strong wet sheet.
